posts.name: Ryan Bender
posts.cooked:
I’m a big fan of Allen Meadows, and access to Burghound has really helped me expand my knowledge of Burgundy and target the wine profiles that I think I’ll like. One area that confuses me is Allen’s use of the word “sauvage” as a descriptor. He generally uses it in a positive way, but what I can’t decide is whether he’s using it to signal earthiness, gaminess, forest floor, or (my worry) barnyard funk. I’m particularly adverse to any kind of barnyard funk in my wine, though I do very much enjoy earthy and forest floor notes, and I don’t at all mind gamy notes in my Rhone reds or Burgundy.

In your experience, do Allen and other wine writers use sauvage to refer to something more akin to barnyard funk, or more of those “wild” notes that might be gamy, forest floor, earthiness, or something else?

posts.name: Patchen Markell
posts.cooked: The Burghound website has a “glossary” (go to the Member/Log In dropdown tab and look for “Glossary.”) I won’t cut & paste here out of respect for his copyright, but the short answer is that he doesn’t mention barnyard in the “sauvage” entry, and does mention the other stuff you list (earthiness, gaminess, forest floor), and I can’t think of instances in which I’ve come across Burgs that he described as “sauvage” or “animale” that smelled specifically like the back end of the animal. (But my sample size is surely smaller than the Burgundy experts here, so take that as one small data point only.)

posts.name: Ryan Bender
posts.cooked: Thank you!! Never thought to check if there was an internal glossary, but that is very helpful.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked: IMG_01691290×1419 249 KB

posts.name: Ryan Bender
posts.cooked: Lol this is the first thing that comes up when you type in the question on Google.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
There is no way to know with certainty - and one person’s ‘sauvage’ may be another person’s ‘barnyard’. . .

Cheers

posts.name: Tony Gibson
posts.cooked: I think sauvage is often used more in the sense of untamed and slightly rambunctious. Maybe a slightly rustic as in it’s a little rough around the edges. It’s a fine line that could be blurred but I think when people like Allen are using French words in an English description they’ll use animale when a wine leans into barnyard territory.

posts.name: Ryan Bender
posts.cooked: That’s what I’m worried about, mainly because Allen does reference “animale” in his glossary, now that I’ve checked that. The animale reference doesn’t necessarily mean anything related to Bar

posts.name: Ryan Bender
posts.cooked: That’s definitely a good point, the only issue being that he does have possible “animale” in his sauvage definition. But that’s also a standalone term he defines, so I think you might be right there.

posts.name: Tony Gibson
posts.cooked: That’s where the fine line is. I think sauvage could include animale but it isn’t necessarily the case. I’m not that familiar with his writing. His experience with the region goes back to when animale was more widespread in the region than it is today so possibly he is less critical of it than some but I imagine if it was an issue he would use language more precise than sauvage to describe it.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
and we wonder why outsiders view wine enthusiasts as pompous / elitist. That we’re struggling to define what it means, shows it as really unsuitable if we care about welcoming people into our hobby. I can just picture someone wafting their hand around whilst putting on an extravagant faux French accent as they say it.

Say what you taste and smell!

posts.name: Ryan Bender
posts.cooked: I don’t really see it as an elitism problem in this case. Every hobby, sport, activity has terms of art that people not in that community don’t use or understand. Terms of art are used to produce a more exact, precise description of something. In this case, it is hard to capture all those scents (forest floor, etc) in one word, and sauvage does a decent job at summing it up (despite my need for clarification on one minor point).

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked: Sometimes we are too close the the hobby to realise how others see it.

posts.name: Ryan Bender
posts.cooked: I suppose that could be right.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
Ryan.B:

I suppose that could be right.

and fwiw I am talking about perception. How many using it are actually being intentionally elitist/obtuse? A very small % IMO.

posts.name: Rob Sutherland
posts.cooked: Soiled leather thong.

posts.name: Bob Hoelting
posts.cooked:
Rob_S1:

Soiled leather thong.

So you’re saying it’s rather monolithic, lacking the context of who wore the thong, at what beach, at what time of day, and whether there’s any hints of sunscreen and street taco.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: It’s an interesting question. Can wine made in clean vessels with clean, ripe grapes ever taste of game birds/animals without the action of spoilage yeasts or bacteria? Certainly, pinot noir can be naturally spicy, especially when vinified with stems; it can be funkily reductive in its youth; and certain barrel toasts can impart a carnal, meaty quality; old barrels can impart woodsy, chestnutty flavors. However I tend to think that it isn’t in the nature of pinot to smell of e.g. hung game bird, no matter where it comes from. This is, of course, at odds with lots of the conventional literature that suggests that e.g. Gevrey should be “wild” (or “sauvage” in French), whatever that means, whereas Chambolle should be floral, and so on. In so far as there is any truth in this (and that is very limited, in my view, as the extraordinarily floral Truchot Charmes 1992 in my glass last weekend would have agreed), I think it comes down to the fact that some terroirs and sites, like some winemaking approaches/traditions, are more susceptible to brettanomyces than others, even if that might strike some terroiristes (especially any who have never made wine or sent it off for analysis) as reductionist.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
TGibson:

I think sauvage is often used more in the sense of untamed and slightly rambunctious. Maybe a slightly rustic as in it’s a little rough around the edges.

That’s what I usually take from it – the opposite of the “elegance” and “refinement” of that eau de toilette.

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

as reductionist.

i appreciate the potential double entendre here.

“she who makes wine in a reductive manner”

posts.name: Wil Raley
posts.cooked:
These are all very valid points, but with Gevrey specifically I find there is a sauvage / game / wild / blood / iron quality that is particularly present that I would have a hard time believing is not tied to terroir, or maybe plant material? Gevrey does have a significant population of old vines (80+ years old) especially when compared to Chambolle.

As an example, this quality shows up in many of Fourrier’s Gevrey wines, especially the Village VV, but not in his Chambolle wines. I’ve also found these qualities in Gevrey based wines from Denis Mortet, Faiveley, Dujac etc. which represents a range of wine making styles. I personally find Gevrey to be one of the easier villages to blind. Maybe it is not always present at the Grand Cru level as these vineyard can be wholly unique in their expression, but for me this seems like an undeniable quality in Gevrey. All that said, I would love to learn more about wine making techniques that lead to this and to be proven wrong!

posts.name: Tony Gibson
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

can be funkily reductive in its youth

I used to sell a Bandol that was sometimes claimed to have too much brett by some pretty good tasters and reviewers. Others like its animale nature. My guess is that there is some brett in there due to old barrels and/or what’s naturally occurring in the vineyard and in the cellar. But I also think the predominate whiff o’ merde that many pick up on is due to Mourvèdre and its proclivity to reduction.
I’ve not run it through a lab test nor do I claim to know what’s really going on. The biggest factor that leads me to think brett is playing only a minor role is that Ia have followed multiple vintages over the course of about 15 years post release and the funky smell never gets worse with age or air. If anything it goes away slightly which leads me to think reduction is the culprit.

William_Kelley:

some terroirs and sites, like some winemaking approaches/traditions,

I’ve had other Bandol with similar percentages of Mourvèdre that are much less tainted, if at all, but I suspect that could just be through a different winemaking technique that lessens reduction.

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked:
Wine critics and writers seem reluctant to call out Brett associated aromas (and flavours), maybe rightly so without an analysis.

Is it enough to say ‘animale’ etc?

TGibson:

I think sauvage is often used more in the sense of untamed and slightly rambunctious. Maybe a slightly rustic as in it’s a little rough around the edges. It’s a fine line that could be blurred but I think when people like Allen are using French words in an English description they’ll use animale when a wine leans into barnyard territory.

agree with untames and rambunctious - for me sauvagehas more to do with the structure than with certain smells like barnyard.
Sauvage is when a wine literally “show its teeth”, when it’s not smooth, balanced and perfectly civilized but has muscles, teeth and strength and a certain nerviness …

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I have only ever heard the term used by French winemakers to refer to aromas/flavors, never structure…

When one is obliged to explain the use of a French word by reference to simpler, easier-to-pin-down English words, it begs the question of whether it might be easier and clearer to just use those English words. Especially if the French word is being used in a sense that isn’t identical with how it is actually used by French speakers.

Wine writing has tended to over-borrow from French, whether it’s <pain grillé> for toast, <sur maturité> for the quite simple but more obviously pejorative over-ripeness, or, my personal favorite, italicized <pigéon> for, you guessed it, pigeon.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

I have only ever heard the term used by French winemakers to refer to aromas/flavors, never structure…

This. I can understand a rough, angular wine being described as rustic, but never sauvage. To me, that term is exclusively for aromatics.

I use the term to deacribe aromatics that most likely come from brett, but may or may not be obviously bretty. I have.no problems calling a bretty wine what it is, but sauvage is a handy term for wines that have some funky, non-fruity elements, but which might not be bretty after all. This term also covers some other off-piste elements like elevated VA, very meaty reductive notes or those very funky notes that might come from aging under flor. Normally I use the term wild for these kinds of wines, but I go for sauvage when I don’t want to get repetitive.

And to me, animale is a very specific kind of funk - and more or less always related to brett.

posts.name: Mikko Tuomi
posts.cooked:
Otto_Forsberg:

And to me, animale is a very specific kind of funk - and more or less always related to brett.

I use a word much more descriptive than that

posts.name: Matthew Benjamin
posts.cooked: You just mean clean visually, right? As in, no wrinkled, blemishes, or rotten grapes?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Yeah, as in devoid of any appreciable quantity of botrytis, downy mildew, powdery mildew, mud, insects, etc etc

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Mikko_Tuomi:
Otto_Forsberg:

And to me, animale is a very specific kind of funk - and more or less always related to brett.

I use a word much more descriptive than that

Could be wet fur not just poop!

I’m smelling that right now on my two golden retrievers, we just walked them down the beach before the big storm comes, but it did get a little drizzly. They both stink. And are covered in sand. That’s animale!!

posts.name: Wil Raley
posts.cooked:
It’s interesting to dig into how different people interpret notes. I would be curious to hear Allen Meadows weigh in on his use the term animale - but I don’t think it’s a polite brett descriptor.

If you’ve ever hunted big game (deer, elk, moose, bear), or smelled a real animal pelt, this descriptor makes significantly more sense because it is in fact, spot on. Hunting animals was once a significantly more common practice, so it’s easy to see why this would be a classic French descriptor. Barnyard is just a nice way of saying poop, and I think it’s a bit of a stretch to say that animals all smell like poop.

Anyway, if you ever have the chance to smell a pelt, I guarantee a lightbulb will go off for certain wines.

posts.name: Patchen Markell
posts.cooked: As long as they didn’t roll around in a dead seal carcass. I smelled dead-seal-on-wet-dog once, 25 years ago, and I’ll never be able to forget it.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
The challenges that we can only describe wines in terms of things that we have experienced ourselves. I have never been around recently butchered animals, and therefore can’t describe wines in those terms. Or in terms of pelts.

And this is why it is impossible to use terms in a universal way . There is always going to be subjectivity involved.

posts.name: Mikko Tuomi
posts.cooked:
I disagree, barnyard and poop are two very different smells, at least for me.
Barnyard can be interesting and somewhat enhances the nose in small quantities, but there are wines where the nose is not barnyard, but actual fecal matter.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: It’s my understanding that volatile phenols, such as those produced by brettanomyces, are actually a big component in the aromatic signatures of game animals. So it is not so much a case of an analogy as of an identity.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: What exactly do you think makes barnyards smell of barnyards?

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

What exactly do you think makes barnyards smell of barnyards?

This.

FWIW, for me “barnyard”, “manure” and “stable floor” always refer to the same thing - horse manure. It’s basically the only agreeable aroma in that genre (although “barnyard” can encompass lots of other aromas, whereas “manure” and “stable floor” are very clear-cut).

When it comes other manure aromas than those of horses; cow manure is quite disagreeable and something I wouldn’t want to smell in a wine; and a wine would be just vile if it smelled of a pigsty.

But if there are no animals in a farm, it really doesn’t smell like barnyard. It just smells of the surrounding nature - usually crop fields. The presence of horses is what makes that barnyardy aroma.

posts.name: Mikko Tuomi
posts.cooked: Combination of manure, hay, decay, lots of things. To me it is very different from what i call poop.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
There are a few interesting pages on animal aromas in Harold McGee’s Nose Dive…

This whole set of animal/decay/manure aromas that you describe is linked to compounds such as skatole, methanethiol, ammonia, cresols, phenols and indole.

Horses in particular produce principally cresols and phenols.

Of these compounds, the volatile phenols are the ones that we most commonly find in red wines, and they are mainly derived from the action of brettanomyces.

posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked: Ooh a great reminder to read this. I acquired a used copy a few years back but it vanished under the ever growing pile marked “to-read”

fancy_title: Clarifying use of the word “sauvage” in wine writing?
title: Clarifying use of the word “sauvage” in wine writing?
